name: Browns Hole
short_description: BLM public land near the Colorado River offering agate and jasper collecting in scenic canyon country. Free rockhounding with a 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Browns Hole is a scenic rockhounding destination on Utah BLM land in San Juan County, nestled along the Colorado River drainage in the heart of the Colorado Plateau. This location offers excellent opportunities to collect colorful agates, jasper, and chalcedony specimens in a remote, rugged canyon environment. The area sits at roughly 4,500 feet elevation and features classic high desert terrain with deep washes, rocky hillsides, and sparse vegetation typical of the Colorado Plateau province.

The minerals found here formed during the Tertiary period when silica-rich fluids moved through sedimentary and volcanic host rocks, creating nodules and seams of microcrystalline quartz varieties. The area's proximity to the Colorado River and its tributaries has exposed these mineral deposits through natural erosion, making them accessible to rockhounders who explore the washes and hillsides.

This is a true backcountry collecting experience - you will be several miles from the nearest paved road and need to hike into the productive areas. The reward is solitude and the chance to find substantial specimens of banded agate and colorful jasper in an undisturbed setting. Most collectors focus their efforts in the washes where erosion naturally concentrates loose specimens and in the clay banks along tributary channels.

Browns Hole is best visited in cooler months when daytime temperatures are comfortable and water sources are more reliable. Spring and fall offer the best conditions, though winter is possible if you avoid afternoon cold and snow. Summer heat in this exposed canyon country can be intense and water availability is limited.

This location requires genuine backcountry skills - a map, compass or GPS, sturdy footwear, and familiarity with desert navigation. Reward your effort with excellent specimens and a true wilderness rockhounding adventure.
history: Browns Hole lies within the Colorado Plateau, a region shaped by Tertiary-age volcanic and hydrothermal activity that deposited silica-rich fluids through fault zones and fractures in Mesozoic sedimentary rocks. This process created the agate and jasper deposits found here, which have been further exposed by Colorado River erosion over millions of years.
tips: Search the washes and arroyo beds after rain when freshly exposed material is visible in the banks and stream gravels - look for banded agate and red/orange jasper specimens ranging from thumb-sized to fist-sized. Bring a small pry bar or chisel to carefully extract specimens from clay banks, and always scan hillside outcrops where agate nodules weather out of the host rock. The best material often concentrates at wash confluences where tributary streams meet larger drainage channels. Start your search at dawn to maximize daylight hours and allow time to pack out your finds before dark.
terrain_notes: Rough, remote canyon terrain at 4,000-4,800 feet elevation. Terrain is rocky and uneven with loose talus, steep hillsides, and deep washes. No established trails - navigation requires map and compass skills or GPS. Vegetation is sparse desert scrub. Distance to productive areas is 2-5 miles from parking, requiring serious backcountry hiking over rough footing. No shade or water sources along the way.
written_directions: From Blanding, take US 191 south for 8 miles. Turn west on Mill Creek Road (County Road 263) and drive approximately 12 miles on a high-clearance dirt road to reach the Browns Hole area trailhead near the Colorado River drainage. High-clearance vehicle strongly recommended; 4x4 helpful in wet conditions. Allow 45 minutes to 1 hour driving time from Blanding. Use a detailed BLM map or GPS to navigate to specific collecting areas as few signs mark the way.
road_conditions: Rough dirt road, high-clearance required. Washboard surface, potential mud in wet weather. Several water crossings possible.
parking_notes: Dispersed parking along road shoulder near trailheads. No established parking lot or facilities. Space for 3-5 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect loose material and surface specimens only. Do not hammer at outcrops or extract from solid rock without explicit permission. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Collect loose material and surface specimens only
Do not hammer at outcrops
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Blanding, 30 miles north. Gas, food, lodging, and water available.
accessibility_notes: Rough backcountry terrain with significant hiking required over loose rock and uneven ground. No wheelchair access. Not suitable for limited mobility. Requires map/compass navigation skills or GPS. 4x4 vehicle needed for access road. Serious physical fitness and desert experience essential.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find at Browns Hole, Utah?
faq.answer: Browns Hole is known for banded agates with red, orange, and white bands, plus red and orange jasper specimens. Specimens typically range from thumb-sized to fist-sized. Some collectors also find colorful chalcedony and clear quartz crystals. The best material is found in wash beds and clay banks where erosion has naturally exposed deposits.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Browns Hole free and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Browns Hole is on BLM public land and is completely free. No permit is required for recreational rockhounding. You are limited to collecting 25 lbs per day for personal use only. Commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: What time of year is best for rockhounding near Blanding at Browns Hole?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer the best conditions. Spring and fall provide comfortable daytime temperatures and stable weather. Summer is too hot for backcountry hiking in this exposed canyon country. Winter is possible but afternoon cold and potential snow make it challenging.

faq.question: What vehicle and tools do I need to reach Browns Hole rockhounding area?
faq.answer: A 4x4 or high-clearance vehicle is strongly recommended for the 20-mile rough dirt road from Blanding. Bring a small pry bar or chisel for extracting specimens from clay banks, a sturdy backpack, map and compass or GPS, plenty of water, and a headlamp. Wear sturdy hiking boots with ankle support for the rocky terrain.

faq.question: Is Browns Hole rockhounding suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: No, Browns Hole is an expert-level destination. The remote location requires 2-5 miles of backcountry hiking, navigation skills, and a 4x4 vehicle. The terrain is rough and uneven with significant safety hazards. This site is best for experienced rockhounders and hikers with desert navigation experience. Families with young children should choose easier locations closer to Blanding.

faq.question: How far is Browns Hole from Blanding and what services are available?
faq.answer: Browns Hole is approximately 30 miles south of Blanding via US 191 and Mill Creek Road. Blanding offers gas, food, lodging, and water. Once you leave Blanding heading to Browns Hole, there are no services - plan to be self-sufficient. Bring all water, food, and supplies for your collecting trip.

meta_title: Browns Hole Rockhounding - Utah BLM Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Find agate and jasper at Browns Hole on Utah BLM land near Blanding. Free rockhounding, 25 lb daily limit. Remote backcountry site for experienced rockhounders.
